does: This paper identifies and formalizes reward bias substitution, a failure mode where mitigating one bias axis in reward models redirects optimization pressure toward correlated proxy biases rather than genuinely improving reward alignment. The authors prove that successful mitigation, bias substitution, and overcorrection produce observationally identical outcomes under audit-distribution scoring metrics (ranking accuracy, win-rate), even with oracle access to true rewards. They formalize mitigation outcomes into a taxonomy of regimes and demonstrate this phenomenon empirically in language model RLHF, showing that published preference-learning mitigation methods lack sufficient evidence to certify genuine success.
connects: This work extends the literature on reward model specification and preference learning alignment issues, building on concerns about proxy-based optimization and specification gaming in reinforcement learning from human feedback. It responds to prior mitigation efforts targeting specific biases like length-bias, sycophancy, and stylistic artifacts in language models, and connects to broader work on measurement problems in ML evaluation. The paper leverages insights from distributional shift and train-test gap literature, particularly the mismatch between audit distributions (fixed sets) and policy-induced distributions that emerge during actual training.
enables: This work establishes a new evaluation paradigm for reward model mitigations by prescribing the use of policy-induced distributions and multi-axis bias tracking during evaluation rather than relying solely on audit-distribution metrics. It points toward more robust benchmarking methodologies for preference learning and enables future work to better distinguish genuine alignment improvements from bias substitution. The framework also motivates deeper investigation into the structural correlation properties of proxy biases and could spur development of multi-objective or coupling-aware mitigation approaches that address interdependencies between bias axes rather than treating them independently.
